title: Getting Started with dsVertClient
text: vertically partitioned setting, different institutions hold different variables set patients. shared patient identifier links records across sites, single institution access complete variable set. dsVertClient extends DataSHIELD run privacy-preserving statistical methods across disjoint columns without ever centralising raw data. goal analyse combined variable space across three institutions without sharing raw data.

title: Getting Started with dsVertClient
text: Build login data frame containing URL, credentials, table reference Opal server, call datashield.login() open connections assign table server-side symbol called "D".
code: library(dsVertClient) library(DSI) library(DSOpal)  builder <- DSI::newDSLoginBuilder() builder$append(server = "server1", url = "https://opal1.example.org",                table = "project.server1_data", user = "analyst",                password = "password", driver = "OpalDriver") builder$append(server = "server2", url = "https://opal2.example.org",                table = "project.server2_data", user = "analyst",                password = "password", driver = "OpalDriver") builder$append(server = "server3", url = "https://opal3.example.org",                table = "project.server3_data", user = "analyst",                password = "password", driver = "OpalDriver")  connections <- datashield.login(builder$build(), assign = TRUE, symbol = "D") Logging into the collaborating servers

title: Methodology
text: protocol uses CKKS scheme (Cheon–Kim–Kim–Song), homomorphic encryption scheme designed approximate arithmetic real numbers. CKKS operates polynomial ring R=ℤ[X]/(XN+1),N=2𝚕𝚘𝚐_𝚗R = \mathbb{Z}[X] / (X^N + 1), \quad N = 2^{\texttt{log\_n}} log_n parameter controls ring dimension. scheme four properties make well suited distributed correlation: SIMD encoding. single ciphertext packs N/2N/2 real-valued slots. log_n = 12 (N=4096N = 4096), ciphertext holds 2048 values, entire column observations fits one ciphertext. Approximate arithmetic. Additions multiplications encrypted data introduce small rounding errors, typically order 10−410^{-4}. negligible statistical correlation. Security. scheme based Ring Learning Errors (RLWE) problem, believed hard even quantum computers (post-quantum candidate). Efficient batching. Element-wise operations full vectors execute single homomorphic operation, avoiding per-element overhead.

title: Methodology
text: Rather entrusting single party secret key, MHE protocol uses additive secret sharing. collective secret key sum KK individual shares: sk=sk1+sk2+…+skKsk = sk_1 + sk_2 + \ldots + sk_K single party holds sksk. Decryption requires KK parties provide partial decryption shares. Even K−1K - 1 colluding parties decrypt ciphertext without remaining party’s cooperation.

Small differences arise three independent sources: CKKS approximation error – homomorphic encryption scheme used cross-server correlation encodes real numbers finite precision. default log_scale = 40, individual operations carry approximately 12 decimal digits precision, accumulation additions inner product results final errors around 10^-4 typical sample sizes. L2 regularisation – BCD algorithm adds small ridge penalty (lambda = 1e-4) diagonal block’s cross-product matrix. prevents singular near-singular systems iterative updates introduces small systematic bias toward zero. BCD convergence tolerance – iterative solver terminates relative change coefficients falls threshold, leaving residual optimisation error negligible practice.

title: Security Model
text: dsVertClient uses Multiparty Homomorphic Encryption (MHE) based CKKS scheme protect data distributed computation. vignette describes threat model, participant learns, security guarantees.

title: Security Model
text: protocol assumes semi-honest (honest--curious) adversary model: parties follow protocol correctly Parties may attempt infer information messages receive party intentionally sends corrupted data deviates protocol standard model federated analytics healthcare research settings, institutions contractually obligated follow protocols may curious others’ data.

title: Security Model
text: distributed correlation, cross-server products computed encrypted data. server encrypts standardized columns collective public key (CPK). single server can decrypt ciphertexts.

title: Security Model
text: response variable y never leaves label server plaintext. Non-label servers compute gradient contributions using encrypted y.

title: Security Model
text: client (researcher coordinating analysis) receives: Collective Public Key (CPK) – decrypt anything alone Encrypted ciphertexts – opaque blobs, undecryptable without K secret key shares Partial decryption shares – share individually reveals nothing Final aggregated results – correlation coefficients, GLM coefficients, deviance client see: Individual-level data server Raw feature values Response variable values Residuals per-observation predictions

title: Security Model
text: Decryption requires cooperation K parties. collective secret key additively shared: sk=sk1+sk2+…+skKsk = sk_1 + sk_2 + \ldots + sk_K party k computes partial decryption share using sk_k. K shares combined can ciphertext decrypted. K--K threshold scheme: every party must participate.

title: Security Model
text: Even K-1 servers collude (share secret key shares), decrypt ciphertext without remaining server’s share. RLWE assumption guarantees partial information K-1 shares computationally indistinguishable random noise. Example 3 servers: Servers 1 2 collude: hold sk_1 + sk_2, need sk_3 Servers 1 3 collude: hold sk_1 + sk_3, need sk_2 3 cooperate: sk_1 + sk_2 + sk_3 = sk, decryption succeeds

title: Multi-Instance Deployment: Three Independent Opal Servers
text: vignette demonstrates dsVert running across three independent Opal/Rock instances, separate host port. Unlike public validation vignette (uses single Opal server three tables), setup mirrors real multi-institution deployment data controller operates infrastructure. analysis identical public validation: PSI alignment, MHE-CKKS correlation, PCA, three GLM families (Gaussian, binomial, Poisson), compared element-wise centralized R outputs.

title: Multi-Instance Deployment: Three Independent Opal Servers
text: server runs independent Opal/Rock instance Docker stack, R session, data store:
code:
library(MASS) data("Pima.tr", package = "MASS") df <- as.data.frame(Pima.tr) df$patient_id <- sprintf("P%03d", seq_len(nrow(df))) df$diabetes   <- ifelse(df$type == "Yes", 1L, 0L)  set.seed(123)  core_ids <- sample(df$patient_id, 150) remaining <- setdiff(df$patient_id, core_ids)  ids1 <- sample(c(core_ids, sample(remaining, 30))) ids2 <- sample(c(core_ids, sample(remaining, 25))) ids3 <- sample(c(core_ids, sample(remaining, 20)))  server1_data <- df[df$patient_id %in% ids1, c("patient_id", "age", "bmi", "ped")] server2_data <- df[df$patient_id %in% ids2, c("patient_id", "glu", "bp", "skin")] server3_data <- df[df$patient_id %in% ids3, c("patient_id", "npreg", "diabetes")]  server1_data <- server1_data[sample(nrow(server1_data)), ] server2_data <- server2_data[sample(nrow(server2_data)), ] server3_data <- server3_data[sample(nrow(server3_data)), ]  cat(sprintf("Server 1: %d patients (age, bmi, ped)
", nrow(server1_data))) #> Server 1: 180 patients (age, bmi, ped) cat(sprintf("Server 2: %d patients (glu, bp, skin)
", nrow(server2_data))) #> Server 2: 175 patients (glu, bp, skin) cat(sprintf("Server 3: %d patients (npreg, diabetes)
", nrow(server3_data))) #> Server 3: 170 patients (npreg, diabetes) cat(sprintf("True intersection: %d patients
",     length(Reduce(intersect, list(ids1, ids2, ids3))))) #> True intersection: 158 patients

title: dsVert Validation: Privacy-Preserving Vertical GLM, Correlation, and PCA
text: birthwt dataset contains 189 observations risk factors low birth weight Baystate Medical Center. split vertically across two hospitals: Hospital : maternal demographics (age, weight, race, smoking) Hospital B: clinical history outcomes (preterm labour, hypertension, uterine irritability, physician visits, birth weight, low birth weight flag)
code: library(MASS) data(birthwt)  df <- birthwt df$patient_id <- sprintf("BW%03d", seq_len(nrow(df)))  set.seed(123) core <- sample(df$patient_id, 160) remaining <- setdiff(df$patient_id, core) ids1 <- sample(c(core, sample(remaining, 15))) ids2 <- sample(c(core, sample(remaining, 10)))  # Hospital A: maternal demographics s1 <- df[df$patient_id %in% ids1, c("patient_id", "age", "lwt", "race", "smoke")] # Hospital B: clinical + outcomes s2 <- df[df$patient_id %in% ids2, c("patient_id", "ptl", "ht", "ui", "ftv", "bwt", "low")]  # Shuffle row order (PSI must handle this) s1 <- s1[sample(nrow(s1)), ] s2 <- s2[sample(nrow(s2)), ]

title: dsVert Validation: Privacy-Preserving Vertical GLM, Correlation, and PCA
code: # Merge as if data were pooled (ground truth) merged <- merge(s1, s2, by = "patient_id")  gt_gaussian <- glm(bwt ~ age + lwt + race + smoke + ptl + ht + ui,                    data = merged, family = gaussian()) gt_binomial <- glm(low ~ age + lwt + race + smoke + ptl + ht + ui,                    data = merged, family = binomial()) gt_poisson  <- glm(ftv ~ age + lwt + race + smoke + ptl + ht + ui,                    data = merged, family = poisson())  summary(gt_binomial)

title: dsVert Validation: Privacy-Preserving Vertical GLM, Correlation, and PCA
text: CGD dataset contains 128 patients clinical trial interferon gamma chronic granulomatous disease (Fleming & Harrington, 1991). split vertically: Hospital : demographics (age, sex, height, weight) Hospital B: treatment assignment (treatment arm, steroid use) Hospital C: outcomes (infection status, total infection count)
code: library(survival) data(cgd)  # One row per patient d <- cgd[cgd$enum == 1, ] d$n_infections <- as.integer(tapply(cgd$status, cgd$id, sum)[as.character(d$id)]) d$patient_id <- sprintf("CGD%03d", seq_len(nrow(d))) d$treat_num <- as.integer(d$treat == "rIFN-g") d$sex_num <- as.integer(d$sex == "male")  # All 128 patients on all servers (different variables) s1 <- d[, c("patient_id", "age", "sex_num", "height", "weight")] s2 <- d[, c("patient_id", "treat_num", "steroids")] s3 <- d[, c("patient_id", "status", "n_infections")]  # Shuffle independently set.seed(42); s1 <- s1[sample(nrow(s1)), ] set.seed(43); s2 <- s2[sample(nrow(s2)), ] set.seed(44); s3 <- s3[sample(nrow(s3)), ]

title: dsVert Validation: Privacy-Preserving Vertical GLM, Correlation, and PCA
code: gt_cgd_gaussian <- glm(weight ~ age + sex_num + height + treat_num + steroids,                        data = d, family = gaussian()) gt_cgd_binomial <- glm(status ~ age + sex_num + height + weight + treat_num + steroids,                        data = d, family = binomial()) gt_cgd_poisson  <- glm(n_infections ~ age + sex_num + height + weight + treat_num + steroids,                        data = d, family = poisson())  summary(gt_cgd_binomial)

title: dsVertClient
text: dsVertClient provides user-friendly R functions privacy-preserving analysis vertically partitioned data across DataSHIELD servers. analyst calls simple functions; cryptographic protocols run transparently.

title: dsVertClient
code: library(DSI); library(DSOpal); library(dsVertClient)  # Connect to servers conns <- DSI::datashield.login(logins = builder$build()) DSI::datashield.assign.table(conns, "D", "project.data")  # 1. Align records (NAs removed automatically, like glm na.omit) ds.psiAlign("D", "patient_id", "DA", datasources = conns)  # 2. Fit GLM using formula (auto-detects which server has each variable) result <- ds.vertGLM(diabetes ~ age + bmi + glu + bp,                      data = "DA", family = "binomial",                      datasources = conns)  # 3. View results (coefficients + SE + p-values + canonical deviance) print(result)  # 4. Correlation + PCA (auto-detects variables) cor <- ds.vertCor("DA", datasources = conns) pca <- ds.vertPCA(cor_result = cor)  DSI::datashield.logout(conns)

title: dsVertClient
text: Zero observation-level disclosure: client sees p-dimensional aggregates Server-generated Beaver triples: client never sees cryptographic material Dealer rotation: different server generates triples iteration (K>=4) Transport encryption: X25519 + AES-256-GCM servers Pure Ring63 MPC: fixed-point additive secret sharing Pinned peers (Ed25519): servers verify ’s identity, preventing Sybil attacks

title: Adaptive Chunking for DataSHIELD Transport — chunk-utils

title: Adaptive Chunking for DataSHIELD Transport — chunk-utils
text: DataSHIELD passes function arguments inline R expressions via HTTP. Large string arguments (base64-encoded ciphertexts, EC points, encrypted vectors) can exceed parser HTTP body size limit. module implements adaptive chunking: Start large initial chunk size (default 100KB, configurable via options(dsvert.chunk_size = N)) first large send, probe whether chunk size accepted probe fails, reduce chunk size 25\ Cache successful chunk size subsequent sends probe conclusive chunk least half target chunk size successfully sent. Small payloads succeed trivially confirm full chunk size accepted.

title: ECDH-PSI Record Alignment (Blind Relay) — ds.psiAlign
text: Privacy-preserving record alignment using Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Private Set Intersection (ECDH-PSI) blind-relay transport encryption. title: ECDH-PSI Record Alignment (Blind Relay) — ds.psiAlign
text: ECDH-PSI exploits commutativity elliptic curve scalar multiplication: \(\alpha \cdot (\beta \cdot H(id)) = \beta \cdot (\alpha \cdot H(id))\). EC point exchanges encrypted server--server (X25519 + AES-256-GCM ECIES). client acts blind relay, seeing opaque blobs. Phase 0: server generates X25519 transport keypair. Public keys exchanged via client. Phase 1: reference server masks IDs scalar \(\alpha\). Points stored server-side (returned client). target server: reference encrypts masked points target's PK. target decrypts, generates scalar \(\beta\), double-masks ref points (stores locally), masks IDs, encrypts ref's PK. reference decrypts, double-masks \(\alpha\), encrypts result target's PK. target decrypts, matches double-masked sets, aligns data. multi-server intersection ensures records present servers retained.

what: Security (DDH assumption on P-256, malicious-client model)
title: ECDH-PSI Record Alignment (Blind Relay) — ds.psiAlign
text: client sees opaque encrypted blobs — EC points. server's scalar never leaves server. PSI firewall: phase ordering + one-shot semantics prevent OPRF oracle attacks.

title: Generalized Linear Model for Vertically Partitioned Data — ds.vertGLM
text: computation uses Ring63 fixed-point arithmetic Beaver MPC: Transport keys: X25519 keypairs servers (~0.5s) Standardize: server standardizes features Input sharing: Features split additive Ring63 shares 2 DCF parties. Non-DCF servers contribute shares. L-BFGS loop: Per iteration: Compute eta shares (Ring63 matrix-vector) DCF wide spline sigmoid/exp (binomial/Poisson) identity link (Gaussian) Beaver matvec gradient (server-generated triples) Client aggregates Ring63 shares -> p gradient scalars L-BFGS quasi-Newton update SE: p+1 gradient evaluations (finite-difference Hessian) Deviance: Beaver dot-product residual sum squares Unstandardize: Coefficients + SE via Jacobian transform

text: Pure Ring63 protocol K>=3 binomial/Poisson GLM. Uses additive secret sharing features 2 DCF parties, DCF wide spline sigmoid/exp, Beaver matvec gradient. Pure Ring63 additive secret sharing.

title: K>=3 Ring63 DCF + Beaver Gradient Loop — k3-ring63-dcf-loop
text: Protocol: One-time: servers share features+y 2 DCF parties (Ring63 FP) Per iteration: DCF parties compute eta X_share*beta DCF wide spline DCF parties -> mu shares Beaver matvec DCF parties -> gradient shares Client aggregates Ring63 shares -> plaintext gradient Client L-BFGS update Requires: transport keys . CPK, Galois, RLK. Precision: ~1e-3 (DCF approximation).
